topic: Is low-skill immigration bad for a country, assuming AGI soon?
current_frame: Single scalar national verdict on low-skill immigration
hard_constraints: Need incidence by group and horizon
Cannot assume local costs equal national welfare loss
Must account for fast automation or AGI as a scenario variable

ideas.title: Ban the scalar verdict
ideas.idea: Model the question as origin x destination x horizon x incidence group instead of one national sign.
ideas.caller_evidence: Directly addresses repeated framing problems in existing immigration memos.

ideas.title: Ban wages as the central metric
ideas.idea: Ask whether immigration improves or worsens a country’s constrained systems: housing, schools, care, logistics, and fiscal coordination.
ideas.caller_evidence: Matches the user’s stated concern about omitted local costs.

ideas.title: Ban current equilibrium assumptions
ideas.paradigm_escaped: Stable labor market and technology baseline
ideas.idea: Run separate ledgers for AGI in 3 years, 10 years, and not soon. The sign may flip by horizon.
ideas.caller_evidence: User explicitly added the AGI-soon assumption.

ideas.title: Treat immigration like surge-capacity management
ideas.idea: Measure whether inflows hit systems with spare capacity or overloaded ones; the same flow can be good in slack regions and bad in brittle ones.
ideas.caller_evidence: Fits the county bridge we just built.

ideas.title: Immigration as option value under deep uncertainty
ideas.idea: Compare immigration against automation, pronatalism, and offshoring as alternative ways to buy labor and demographic option value.
ideas.caller_evidence: No direct incident, but useful for AGI-era framing.

ideas.title: Look for bottleneck relief, not average output
ideas.idea: Evaluate whether low-skill immigration unclogs high-leverage bottlenecks like elder care, food processing, warehousing, or housing construction.
ideas.caller_evidence: Explains why some sectors may benefit even if broad averages mislead.

ideas.title: Study carrying capacity and niche overlap
ideas.idea: Estimate where immigrant labor overlaps directly with low-skill natives versus where it occupies underfilled niches.
ideas.caller_evidence: Important but already common in labor literature; lower novelty.

ideas.title: Assume housing supply is nearly fixed
ideas.idea: Under near-fixed supply, the right question is who bears crowding and rent stress, not whether GDP rose.
ideas.caller_evidence: Directly connected to the CHAS housing-stress layer.

ideas.title: Assume robots become cheaper than people soon
ideas.transfer_mechanism: Compare imported labor to imported capital
ideas.idea: Ask whether a country should import low-skill workers or accelerate automation and capital deepening instead.

ideas.title: Assume local budgets cannot smooth shocks
ideas.paradigm_escaped: Federal and local budgets effectively pooled
ideas.transfer_mechanism: Force fiscal mismatch into the core model
ideas.idea: Treat federal-positive and local-negative splits as a first-class result, not an afterthought.
ideas.caller_evidence: This is already the strongest pattern in the project.

ideas.title: Assume descendant outcomes improve under AI tutoring
ideas.transfer_mechanism: Reopen the intergenerational channel under AGI
ideas.idea: If AI sharply compresses skill acquisition costs, descendants may matter more positively than older fiscal models assume.

ideas.title: Ban the nation-state average entirely
ideas.paradigm_escaped: Country as the unit of moral and economic analysis
ideas.idea: Judge immigration by whether institutions remain functional: school quality, public order, housing adequacy, fiscal solvency, and political legitimacy.
ideas.caller_evidence: Closer to what the user actually means by bad for a country.

ideas.title: Measure employer pass-through versus rent capture
ideas.paradigm_escaped: Consumer gains assumed to diffuse broadly
ideas.idea: Estimate how much of the gain from cheaper labor goes to consumers versus employers, landlords, and incumbent capital.
ideas.caller_evidence: Critical for testing the Clark-style average-citizen claim.

ideas.title: Ban immigrant-only framing
ideas.idea: Compare low-skill immigration against pronatalism, internal mobility, automation, training, offshoring, and higher labor-force participation by natives.
ideas.caller_evidence: Without a counterfactual the verdict is underspecified.
